summary:
Akbar the Great (r. 1556–1605 AD), the third [[event:103|Mughal Emperor]], was the greatest ruler of the dynasty and one of the most capable monarchs of any era, who transformed a shaky inherited kingdom into a consolidated empire covering most of northern and central India through military conquest combined with a distinctive policy of religious tolerance, administrative innovation, and cultural synthesis that made his reign a golden age of Indian civilization. Ascending the throne at 14 after the sudden death of [[event:3233|Humayun]], Akbar's early reign was guided by his regent Bairam Khan; he assumed personal control at 18 and spent the next two decades systematically defeating rivals — Hemu's Hindu Samrat bid at the Second Battle of Panipat (1556), the Rajput fortress of Chittor (1568), and Ranthambore (1569) — while simultaneously implementing the administrative and political framework that would sustain Mughal power.

Akbar's defining achievement was his policy of sulh-i-kul ('universal peace' or 'peace with all') — a deliberate religious eclecticism that abolished the jizya (poll tax on non-Muslims), appointed Hindu Rajputs to the highest military and administrative positions, married Rajput princesses (creating political alliances with the major Hindu kingdoms), and engaged in genuine intellectual curiosity about all religions including Christianity, Zoroastrianism, Jainism, and Hinduism. At his court discussions (ibadatkhana), scholars of all faiths debated theology while Akbar listened and questioned. His administrative system — the mansabdar ranking structure tying noble status and salary to military obligation, the Ain-i-Akbari administrative manual by his court chronicler Abu'l-Fazl, standardized land revenues (Todar Mal's Ain) — created the institutional foundation for Mughal governance that persisted for a century. The new capital Fatehpur Sikri, built in the 1570s near Agra, embodied the architectural synthesis of Mughal, Rajput, and Hindu traditions that characterized his reign.
